title: US-Iran Deal Nears as Hormuz Reopens and Oil Markets Hold Steady
subhead: A tentative ceasefire agreement would reopen the Strait of Hormuz and dismantle Iran's nuclear program, while OPEC output hits two-decade lows and DRC's cobalt ban reshapes battery supply chains.

sections.heading: US-Iran Deal Nears Completion
sections.body:
The United States and Iran are close to signing an agreement to end their four-month war, according to regional officials speaking to the Associated Press. Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi posted on X that an agreement 'has never been closer,' while US President Donald Trump shared the message and said a deal could be signed within days. A senior US official told AP the tentative deal includes removal and destruction of Iran's nuclear material and dismantling of Tehran's nuclear program, along with commitments not to fund terrorist groups.

The emerging agreement, brokered primarily by Pakistan with backing from Saudi Arabia, Turkey, Egypt, and Qatar, would pave the way for reopening the Strait of Hormuz, phased lifting of sanctions on Iran, and release of frozen Iranian assets. However, the deal remains fragile — Trump publicly lashed out at Iranian officials on Friday, warning 'they better get their act together, and FAST!' Israel, which says it is not a party to the negotiations, expects Trump to uphold key Israeli interests including weakening Iran's missile program and proxy network.
sections.whyItMatters: A successful deal would end the most serious oil supply disruption in decades, but the performance-based structure requiring verified nuclear dismantlement before sanctions relief creates multiple potential failure points.

sections.heading: Hormuz Flow Increases as US Escorts Tankers
sections.body:
Iran's stranglehold on the Strait of Hormuz appears to be loosening as more Gulf Arab oil reaches global markets. Kpler, a commodities monitoring firm, has tracked approximately 96 million barrels of non-Iranian crude exports getting out of the region since early May, either through the strait or via Gulf of Oman export options — broadly consistent with Trump's claim of over 100 million barrels moved through a 'secret mission.' US forces are conducting limited overwatch operations using autonomous vehicles, aircraft and drone escorts to help ships through the southern part of the strait near Oman's coast.

The US has simultaneously maintained a naval blockade on Iranian ports, shooting at or stopping ships associated with Iran's sanctioned oil trade. Wood Mackenzie estimates Iran's output has fallen by 800,000 barrels per day since the blockade began. Iran is topping out onshore storage and using dozens of tankers off Kharg Island to store crude. The country's stored oil is estimated at 69 million barrels, the highest level since Trump's 'maximum pressure' campaign in 2020.
sections.whyItMatters: The dual approach — escorting friendly tankers while blockading Iranian exports — has given the US leverage in negotiations while preventing a full-blown global energy crisis, but it's not sustainable long-term.

sections.heading: US Becomes World's Top Oil Exporter
sections.body:
The United States has surpassed Saudi Arabia and Russia to become the world's largest oil exporter, according to Reuters. The milestone comes as the Iran war reshapes global energy trade — Hormuz closures have slashed Gulf exports while American shale production continues to surge. The shift tightens American companies' grip on energy markets at a moment when Washington is actively using energy as a geopolitical tool.

OPEC oil output in May hit its lowest level since at least 2000, a Reuters survey found, as the US blockade cut Iran's exports and Hormuz closures reduced exports by other Gulf producers. OPEC+ has approved four oil output quota hikes since the Hormuz closure, but several members remain unable to pump more due to the conflict. Saudi Arabia and neighboring Gulf producers will welcome the eventual reopening of Hormuz, but the ensuing flood of oil risks eroding OPEC's already fragile grip on the market.
sections.whyItMatters: The Iran war has accelerated a structural shift in global oil power toward the US, potentially weakening OPEC's market influence for years to come.

sections.heading: DRC Cobalt Export Ban Reshapes Battery Supply Chains
sections.body:
The Democratic Republic of Congo's cobalt export restrictions have sent prices past all recorded benchmarks, with cobalt metal prices more than doubling from near nine-year lows of $21,502 per tonne to $48,570 by October 2025. The DRC imposed an export ban on cobalt hydroxide in February 2025, later replacing it with strict quotas after years of low prices and market glut left the country's dominant resource undervalued. The Cobalt Institute recorded a 130% rise in refined metal prices, with hydroxide prices quadrupling.

Chinese refiners, who process the bulk of Congolese hydroxide, are now turning to shredded batteries (black mass) and recycled cobalt as substitutes. However, production of Mixed Hydroxide Precipitate (MHP), an alternative feedstock, is also expected to fall because the Iran war has triggered a global shortage of sulfuric acid used in its production. India, which is 100% import-dependent for lithium, cobalt and nickel according to the Institute for Energy Economics and Financial Analysis, faces particular exposure as its EV sales reached 2.5 million units in FY2026.

The supply squeeze is accelerating a shift toward cobalt-free LFP (lithium iron phosphate) batteries. Morgan Stanley analysts noted that LFP batteries are 'less exposed to nickel and cobalt supply risks,' and LFP cells continued their rapid ascent in 2025, driven by cost advantages and growing adoption in China.
sections.whyItMatters: DRC's policy has exposed the vulnerability of concentrated critical mineral supply chains and accelerated the industry's move away from cobalt — a structural change that will outlast the current price spike.

sections.heading: Ukraine's Drone Campaign Creates Crimea Fuel Crisis
sections.body:
Ukrainian drone strikes on fuel supplies have triggered the worst fuel crisis in Russian-occupied Crimea since 2014, according to Reuters and regional media. Fuel stations on the peninsula ran dry on Thursday after a fresh night of Ukrainian drone strikes. Residents are grappling with gasoline rationing after Ukrainian attacks constricted supplies from Russia. The campaign has methodically targeted fuel supplies, refineries, and the Kerch Bridge linking Crimea to Russia.

Reuters reported that Russian-held Crimea faces severe fuel shortages, with residents describing the situation as dire. The strikes represent a new Ukrainian strategy to turn Putin's main supply line into what one report called 'a road of ruin,' targeting logistics rather than front-line positions. The fuel crisis threatens both Russian military logistics and the peninsula's tourism-dependent economy as summer approaches.
sections.whyItMatters: Ukraine's asymmetric campaign demonstrates how drone warfare can disrupt occupying forces' energy logistics, creating strategic pressure without large-scale offensive operations.

sections.heading: Nigeria Battles Pipeline Vandalism as Output Rises
sections.body:
Nigeria's crude oil production surpassed its OPEC quota in May 2026, reaching a 15-month high of 1.53 million barrels per day, according to OPEC data. The production gains come as the Nigerian National Petroleum Company Limited intensifies its fight against pipeline vandalism. NNPC reported 24 pipeline theft incidents since 2025, including a case where criminals posing as a federal government task force stole about nine kilometers of pipelines across the network.

Security agencies have generated valuable leads regarding the sponsors and receivers of vandalized pipelines. Nigeria's ability to maintain production above its OPEC quota of 1.5 million bpd represents a turnaround for a country long plagued by infrastructure attacks and oil theft. The industry-wide security architecture has deepened collaboration between NNPC and security agencies to protect energy infrastructure.
sections.whyItMatters: Nigeria's production gains show that security investments can pay off, but the sophisticated nature of pipeline theft — including impersonation of government task forces — indicates ongoing risks to African energy supply.

sections.heading: China-North Korea Tumen River Talks Stall
sections.body:
A tiny river on Russia's border is testing China's relationship with North Korea, according to the South China Morning Post. The Tumen River, a natural border between China, North Korea, and Russia, blocks Chinese access to the Sea of Japan — a narrow strip running between North Korea and Russia prevents Beijing from reaching open waters. China has long tried to convince its neighbors to open the waterway to Chinese traffic, and last month there were small positive signs from Moscow.

However, after this week's summit between Chinese leader Xi Jinping and North Korean leader Kim Jong-un, official statements made no mention of the issue. The silence points to continued wariness in Pyongyang and Moscow about Beijing's influence in the region. Since ceding territory that includes Primorsky Krai to the Russian empire in 1860, Beijing has pursued direct access to the Sea of Japan via the Tumen.
sections.whyItMatters: Access to the Sea of Japan would give China an alternative energy shipping route and reduce dependence on Hormuz and Malacca chokepoints — but regional geopolitics keep the waterway closed.

bottomLine: The tentative US-Iran agreement, if finalized, would reopen Hormuz and reshape global energy flows after four months of disruption. But the bigger story is structural: US shale has displaced Saudi Arabia and Russia as the top exporter, DRC's cobalt policy is forcing a permanent shift away from cobalt in battery chemistry, and the weaponization of energy infrastructure — from Hormuz to Crimea to Nigeria's pipelines — is now a standard feature of modern conflict. The energy security playbook is being rewritten.
sources: AP News, Reuters, Anadolu Agency, South China Morning Post, News18, Kpler, Wood Mackenzie, Cobalt Institute, Institute for Energy Economics and Financial Analysis, OPEC, Punch Nigeria, Arise TV
